title: 03 - Inflammation and Repair - Exam Prep Questions
description: Third-party generated single-choice exam-prep questions for Chapter 3. The set is aligned primarily to the Chapter 3 textbook content and shaped to reflect the style, tone, and difficulty distribution of the RCPA Basic Pathological Sciences mock exam format. Choices are keyed so answers remain stable if an application randomizes display order.

questions.question:
A 54-year-old man with recurrent gout develops abrupt, intensely painful inflammation in the first metatarsophalangeal joint. No organisms are identified.

What mechanism MOST directly explains leukocyte recruitment in this sterile inflammatory reaction?

choices.text: Urate crystals activate the inflammasome, leading to IL-1 production
Immune complexes activate mast cells exclusively through IgE
Fibroblasts release TGF-beta to suppress neutrophil migration
Loss of platelets prevents complement activation
Reduced endothelial permeability allows plasma proteins to escape

answer.reason: Damage-associated molecular patterns such as urate crystals activate the inflammasome and promote IL-1 production, which recruits leukocytes.

questions.question:
A patient with congestive heart failure develops ankle edema. Aspirated fluid shows very low protein content and few cells.

How should this fluid be classified?

answer.reason: A transudate has low protein, few cells, and results from hydrostatic or osmotic imbalance without increased vascular permeability.

questions.question:
A teenager develops a skin blister after a minor burn. The blister cavity contains cell-poor fluid with few leukocytes.

Which morphologic pattern of acute inflammation is MOST likely?

answer.reason: Serous inflammation is characterized by cell-poor fluid accumulation in spaces created by injury to surface epithelia or serous cavities.

questions.question:
A patient develops rapid swelling at the site of a bee sting within minutes. The vascular leak is mainly due to endothelial cell retraction in postcapillary venules.

Which mediator is MOST strongly associated with this immediate transient response?

answer.reason: Histamine is the principal mediator of the immediate transient increase in vascular permeability caused by endothelial retraction.

questions.question: During leukocyte recruitment, which family of adhesion molecules is primarily responsible for the initial rolling interaction on activated endothelium?

answer.reason: Selectins mediate the weak, reversible rolling interactions that slow leukocytes along activated endothelium.

questions.question:
A neutrophil has already adhered firmly to activated endothelium and is about to squeeze between endothelial cells in a postcapillary venule.

Which molecule is MOST important for this transmigration step?

answer.reason: PECAM-1 mediates the binding events needed for leukocytes to traverse endothelium during transmigration.

questions.question:
A patient with bacterial peritonitis is forming a strong chemotactic gradient for neutrophils.

Which of the following is a well-established leukocyte chemoattractant in this setting?

answer.reason: Leukotriene B4 is a potent chemoattractant for neutrophils and also activates leukocyte adhesion and effector responses.

questions.question:
A lung biopsy taken 12 hours after acute bacterial infection shows a dense neutrophilic infiltrate. A second biopsy 48 hours later shows mainly monocyte-derived macrophages.

What BEST explains this shift?

choices.text: Macrophages are more numerous than neutrophils in blood
Macrophages survive longer in tissues and may proliferate locally
Neutrophils require antibody to leave vessels
Neutrophils convert directly into macrophages in tissue
Platelets replace neutrophils during chronic inflammation

answer.reason: Neutrophils dominate early because they respond rapidly and are numerous, but macrophages become dominant later because they survive longer and may proliferate in tissues.

questions.question: A patient with recurrent bacterial infection has defective opsonization. Which molecule normally enhances microbial recognition and attachment during phagocytosis?

answer.reason: C3b is a major opsonin that binds microbial surfaces and facilitates recognition and attachment by phagocytes.

questions.question: Within an activated neutrophil phagolysosome, which enzyme converts hydrogen peroxide into hypochlorite for efficient microbial killing?

answer.reason: Myeloperoxidase uses hydrogen peroxide and halide ions to generate hypochlorite, one of the most effective neutrophil microbicidal products.

questions.question: A patient with systemic lupus erythematosus has high titers of antibodies against nuclear material. Which neutrophil response can directly expose chromatin and histones to the immune system?

answer.reason: NET formation releases chromatin and associated proteins into extracellular spaces, creating a potential source of autoantigens.

questions.question:
A patient with acute inflammation has marked pain and increased vascular permeability mediated by a plasma-derived peptide generated from high-molecular-weight kininogen.

Which mediator is MOST likely responsible?

answer.reason: Bradykinin is derived from kininogens and increases vascular permeability, causes vasodilation, and contributes to pain.

questions.question:
A patient starts a selective COX-2 inhibitor and later suffers an arterial thrombotic event.

Which imbalance BEST explains this complication?

choices.text: Decreased PGI2 with relatively preserved TXA2
Decreased TXA2 with relatively preserved PGI2
Increased lipoxin with decreased leukotriene B4

answer.reason: Selective COX-2 inhibition may reduce endothelial prostacyclin while leaving platelet TXA2 production relatively intact, favoring thrombosis.

questions.question:
A patient with asthma improves on montelukast therapy.

Which inflammatory mediators are MOST directly blocked by this drug class?

answer.reason: Leukotriene receptor antagonists such as montelukast block the cysteinyl leukotrienes that cause bronchospasm and increased vascular permeability.

questions.question:
A woman with rheumatoid arthritis is treated with a TNF antagonist and later develops reactivated tuberculosis.

What is the MOST likely immunologic basis for this complication?

choices.text: Failure to maintain effective macrophage-mediated containment of intracellular microbes
Loss of platelet aggregation in granulomas

answer.reason: TNF is important for macrophage activation and the containment of intracellular pathogens such as mycobacteria, so antagonism increases tuberculosis risk.

questions.question: Which infection is classically associated with deficiency of the terminal complement components because of impaired membrane attack complex formation?

answer.reason: The membrane attack complex is especially important for killing Neisseria, so terminal complement deficiency predisposes to gonococcal and meningococcal infection.

questions.question:
A circulating host protein binds microbial mannose residues and directly activates complement without antibody.

Which pathway is involved?

answer.reason: The lectin pathway is activated when mannose-binding lectin binds microbial carbohydrates and directly triggers complement activation.

questions.question:
A patient with acute appendicitis undergoes surgery. The specimen shows liquefied necrotic debris, edema fluid, and large numbers of neutrophils.

Which morphologic pattern is MOST likely present?

answer.reason: Purulent inflammation is defined by pus composed of neutrophils, liquefied necrotic debris, and edema fluid.

questions.question:
A healthy adult donates part of the liver for transplantation. Over the next several weeks, the remaining liver increases in mass.

What is the PRIMARY mechanism of this restoration in humans after partial hepatectomy?

choices.text: Proliferation of residual hepatocytes driven by cytokines and growth factors
Replacement by permanent scar tissue only

answer.reason: After partial hepatectomy, liver restoration occurs mainly by proliferation of residual hepatocytes under the influence of cytokines such as IL-6 and growth factors such as HGF.

questions.question:
A fibrinous pleural exudate persists because it cannot be cleared adequately.

What is the MOST likely long-term outcome?

choices.text: Organization with ingrowth of fibroblasts and scar formation
Transformation into a foreign body granuloma

answer.reason: When fibrinous exudate is not removed, fibroblasts and vessels grow into it, producing organization and scar formation.

questions.question:
A biopsy from chronically inflamed tissue shows macrophages, lymphocytes, and plasma cells, together with tissue destruction and fibrosis.

Which term BEST describes this process?

answer.reason: Chronic inflammation is defined by mononuclear infiltrates, tissue destruction, and ongoing repair with fibrosis and angiogenesis.

questions.question:
A macrophage population activated mainly by IL-4 and IL-13 is identified in a healing wound.

What is the MOST characteristic function of these cells?

choices.text: Generation of the strongest respiratory burst for bacterial killing
Promotion of tissue repair, angiogenesis, and collagen deposition
Release of histamine from cytoplasmic granules

answer.reason: IL-4 and IL-13 drive alternative macrophage activation, whose major functions are tissue repair, fibroblast activation, angiogenesis, and collagen synthesis.

questions.question:
A patient with a chronic inflammatory disorder has increased activity of TH17 cells.

What effect is MOST directly associated with this T-cell subset?

choices.text: Activation of mast-cell histamine release only
Induction of chemokines that recruit neutrophils and monocytes
Production of type I interferons to block viral replication
Promotion of platelet aggregation by TXA2
Suppression of endothelial adhesion molecule expression

answer.reason: TH17 cells promote recruitment of neutrophils and monocytes by inducing chemokine secretion.

questions.question: A child with helminth infection has marked eosinophilia. Which eosinophil granule protein is especially important for parasite toxicity but can also injure host epithelium?

answer.reason: Eosinophil granules contain major basic protein, which is toxic to parasites but can also damage host epithelial cells.

questions.question:
A pathologist sees multinucleated giant cells and epithelioid macrophages surrounding suture material, without evidence of a strong T-cell-driven immune response.

What type of granuloma is this?

answer.reason: Foreign body granulomas form around relatively inert material that cannot be phagocytosed and do not require a strong T-cell-mediated response.

questions.question:
A lung biopsy shows granulomas with central caseous necrosis.

Which diagnosis must always be excluded first when this pattern is identified?

answer.reason: Tuberculosis is the prototype granulomatous disease caused by infection and must be excluded whenever granulomas are identified, especially if caseating.

questions.question:
A patient with influenza develops fever during the systemic acute-phase response.

Which mechanism BEST explains the rise in body temperature?

choices.text: Bradykinin directly resets the hypothalamic set point
C3b deposits on erythrocytes and triggers shivering
Cytokine-induced PGE2 production in hypothalamic vascular and perivascular cells raises the set point
IL-4 suppresses heat loss through sweat glands
Leukotriene B4 activates skeletal muscle to generate heat

answer.reason: Fever in the acute-phase response is driven by cytokine-induced hypothalamic prostaglandin synthesis, especially PGE2.

questions.question:
A patient with bacterial pneumonia has a markedly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate.

Which acute-phase protein MOST directly promotes rouleaux formation and faster sedimentation of red cells?

answer.reason: Fibrinogen promotes rouleaux formation, which increases erythrocyte sedimentation rate during the acute-phase response.

questions.question:
A healing skin wound is generating new capillary sprouts from preexisting vessels.

Which factor is the key driver of endothelial migration, proliferation, and increased permeability in this process?

answer.reason: VEGF is the key driver of angiogenesis in repair, stimulating endothelial migration, proliferation, vasodilation, and permeability.

questions.question:
A patient with long-standing chronic liver injury develops progressive fibrosis and architectural distortion.

Which mediator is MOST important in driving fibroblast activation, collagen synthesis, and reduced extracellular matrix degradation in this setting?

answer.reason: TGF-beta is the major fibrogenic cytokine and stimulates fibroblast migration, collagen deposition, and decreased matrix degradation in fibrosis.
